question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: convertTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ConsensusTopicQuery with consensusStartTime.seconds set to exactly 9223372035L to force convertTimestamp() to return Long.MAX_VALUE as startTime, causing the TopicMessageFilter to skip all historical messages and present a falsely empty or truncated topic history to any observer replaying the stream?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: convertTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a consensusStartTime with seconds=9223372034L and nanos=999999999 to produce a nanosecond timestamp just below the Long.MAX_VALUE boundary, causing DomainUtils.timestampInNanosMax() to overflow and wrap to a negative startTime, resulting in the filter returning messages out of their true chronological order?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: convertTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user set consensusEndTime.seconds to 9223372035L or above so that convertTimestamp() returns Long.MAX_VALUE as endTime, effectively removing the end-time boundary and causing the subscription to stream all future messages indefinitely, distorting the bounded historical window that other clients rely on for audit purposes?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user omit the consensusStartTime field entirely in ConsensusTopicQuery so that toFilter() leaves startTime at the TopicMessageFilter default (DomainUtils.now()), causing the subscription to silently skip all historical messages and present a history that begins at the subscription moment rather than the true topic genesis?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user set consensusStartTime to a future timestamp that passes the isValidStartTime() check boundary (startTime <= DomainUtils.now()) by a race condition in clock skew, causing the filter to be built with a future startTime that skips currently-existing messages and misrepresents the topic's message history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user set limit=0 in ConsensusTopicQuery so that TopicMessageFilter.hasLimit() returns false, causing the subscription to stream an unbounded number of messages and bypass any intended pagination boundary that would otherwise enforce a consistent historical view?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user set limit=Long.MAX_VALUE in ConsensusTopicQuery to cause TopicMessageFilter to hold an astronomically large limit, exhausting server-side resources and causing the stream to terminate prematurely, resulting in an incomplete and misleading historical record delivered to the client?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user omit the topicID field in ConsensusTopicQuery so that toFilter() calls filter.topicId() with a null EntityId, causing a NullPointerException or validation error that is mapped by onErrorMap(ProtoUtil::toStatusRuntimeException) to a generic INVALID_ARGUMENT status, leaking no useful error but also silently aborting a legitimate subscriber's history replay?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a topicID with shard=0, realm=0, num=0 (the zero EntityId) to toFilter(), causing EntityId.of() to produce an entity that matches no real topic or matches a system-reserved entity, resulting in an empty or incorrect history stream being returned as if it were a valid topic's history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toFilter()] Can an unprivileged external user set consensusStartTime equal to consensusEndTime in ConsensusTopicQuery, causing isValidEndTime() (endTime > startTime) to fail with a ConstraintViolationException that is mapped to INVALID_ARGUMENT, but in doing so the attacker can probe exact nanosecond boundaries of the message history to infer the precise timestamps of specific messages?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toResponse()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that toResponse() uses Objects.requireNonNullElse(t.getRunningHashVersion(), DEFAULT_RUNNING_HASH_VERSION) where DEFAULT_RUNNING_HASH_VERSION=3, and by subscribing to a topic whose stored runningHashVersion is null (legacy blockstream data), receive responses that falsely advertise hash version 3, causing clients to validate running hashes with the wrong algorithm and accept a corrupted or reordered history as valid?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toResponse()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that toResponse() sets runningHash via ProtoUtil.toByteString(t.getRunningHash()) without any server-side integrity verification, so that if the underlying TopicMessage.runningHash field is null, the response emits ByteString.EMPTY as the running hash, causing clients to accept a message with no hash proof and misinterpret the topic's integrity chain?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toResponse()] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a topic and observe that toResponse() emits sequenceNumber directly from t.getSequenceNumber() with no gap detection at the controller layer, so that if the database contains messages with non-contiguous sequence numbers (due to a prior ingestion bug), the client receives a stream that appears to have gaps, making the history appear reorganized or incomplete?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toResponse()] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a topic and observe that toResponse() emits consensusTimestamp via ProtoUtil.toTimestamp(t.getConsensusTimestamp()) without verifying monotonic ordering, so that if two messages share the same consensusTimestamp (a database anomaly), the client receives duplicate timestamps and cannot determine the true ordering of those messages?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toResponse()] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a chunked-message topic and observe that toResponse() sets chunkInfo.number=t.getChunkNum() and chunkInfo.total=t.getChunkTotal() without validating that chunkNum <= chunkTotal, so that a stored message with chunkNum > chunkTotal causes clients to misassemble the chunked message and reconstruct a different payload than what was originally submitted?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: parseTransactionID()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the parseTransactionID() silent-failure path by subscribing to a topic that contains a message with a malformed initialTransactionId byte array, causing the function to log an error and return null, so that the chunkInfo.initialTransactionID is omitted from the response and clients cannot trace the chunk back to its originating transaction, effectively hiding the transaction origin in the history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: parseTransactionID()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that parseTransactionID() catches InvalidProtocolBufferException and returns null without propagating the error to the client, so that a subscriber receives a chunkInfo block with no initialTransactionID and no indication of the parse failure, causing the client to silently accept an incomplete history record as authoritative?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toResponse()] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a topic and observe that when parseTransactionID() returns null and payerAccountEntity is also null, toResponse() builds a chunkInfo with no initialTransactionID at all, causing the chunk's provenance to be completely absent from the response and making it impossible for clients to verify the chunk's place in the transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: toResponse()] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a topic and observe that when parseTransactionID() returns null but payerAccountEntity is non-null and validStartInstant is non-null, toResponse() constructs a synthetic TransactionID from payerAccountEntity.toAccountID() and validStartTimestamp, potentially producing a TransactionID that differs from the original on-chain transaction ID, causing clients to associate the chunk with the wrong transaction in their history reconstruction?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribeTopic()] Can an unprivileged external user open a large number of concurrent subscribeTopic() connections to the same topic with overlapping time ranges, causing the server to emit duplicate or interleaved message streams to different subscribers, and by comparing the streams, infer inconsistencies in the historical ordering that reveal database-level race conditions?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribeTopic()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that subscribeTopic() uses Mono.fromCallable(() -> toFilter(request)).flatMapMany(topicMessageService::subscribeTopic) without any rate limiting or connection throttling, allowing the attacker to flood the server with subscription requests that each query overlapping historical windows, causing resource exhaustion that disrupts legitimate subscribers' ability to receive a consistent history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribeTopic()] Can an unprivileged external user cancel a subscribeTopic() stream immediately after receiving the first few messages (triggering serverCallStreamObserver.setOnCancelHandler(disposable::dispose)), then re-subscribe with a startTime just after the last received message, and by repeating this pattern rapidly, observe whether the server returns different orderings of messages at the boundary, revealing non-deterministic history delivery?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: subscribeTopic()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the onErrorMap(ProtoUtil::toStatusRuntimeException) mapping to distinguish between different internal server error types (DEADLINE_EXCEEDED vs RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED vs UNAVAILABLE) by crafting queries that trigger specific exceptions, and use this oracle to infer the internal state of the message history database?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: convertTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user set consensusStartTime.nanos to a negative value (which protobuf allows as an int32) to cause DomainUtils.timestampInNanosMax() to compute a startTime that is earlier than the seconds field alone would suggest, causing the filter to return messages from before the intended start boundary and presenting a history window that is wider than the client requested?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/controller/ConsensusController.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: convertTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user set consensusStartTime.nanos=999999999 and seconds just below the 9223372035L threshold to produce a nanosecond timestamp that is within one nanosecond of Long.MAX_VALUE, causing DomainUtils.timestampInNanosMax() to return Long.MAX_VALUE due to overflow, and thereby causing the startTime filter to skip all historical messages?
